Since 16 four-byte integers fit in one 64-byte cache line, up to 16 threads would false-share. The fix is to pad each counter to 64 bytes using alignas(64) or a struct with explicit padding, ensuring each counter occupies its own cache line.

rubric.key: Correctly defines false sharing in terms of cache lines
Provides a concrete game engine example
Describes the fix (cache-line padding or data restructuring)

questions.question: In a job-based multithreading system, explain how declaring read/write dependencies between jobs enables safe parallelism without locks. Give an example with three game systems.
questions.answer: Each job declares which component types it reads and which it writes. The scheduler analyzes these declarations: jobs that access disjoint data can run in parallel, while jobs that share data (one writes, another reads the same component) must run sequentially. Example: PhysicsJob reads VelocityComponent and writes PositionComponent. AIJob reads PositionComponent (to know where entities are) and writes AIStateComponent. RenderPrepJob reads PositionComponent and SpriteComponent and writes RenderCommandBuffer. PhysicsJob and AIJob cannot run in parallel because Physics writes PositionComponent while AI reads it. But AIJob and RenderPrepJob also cannot parallelize since RenderPrep reads Position that Physics modifies. The schedule becomes: PhysicsJob first, then AIJob and RenderPrepJob in parallel (AI reads Position but does not conflict with RenderPrep because they write to different components). No locks are needed because the schedule guarantees no concurrent read-write conflicts.

rubric.key: Explains the read/write dependency declaration mechanism
Provides three concrete game systems with correct dependencies
Shows which jobs can parallelize and which must be sequential

quizzes.topic: Networking, Client-Side Prediction, and State Synchronization

questions.question: Explain client-side prediction and server reconciliation in a networked game. Why is client-side prediction necessary, and what happens when the server's authoritative state disagrees with the client's predicted state?
questions.answer: Client-side prediction is necessary because network round-trip times (50-200ms) would make controls feel unresponsive if the client waited for server confirmation before moving. Instead, the client immediately applies input locally and stores a buffer of recent inputs with sequence numbers. When the server sends back its authoritative state tagged with the last processed input sequence number, the client compares its predicted state at that sequence to the server's state. If they match, no correction is needed. If they disagree, the client snaps to the server state and re-applies all inputs that occurred after the server's confirmed sequence number, effectively replaying unconfirmed inputs on top of the corrected state. This correction is smoothed over 100-200ms to prevent visible teleportation.

rubric.key: Explains why prediction is needed (latency hiding)
Describes input buffer with sequence numbers
Explains reconciliation: snap to server state and replay unconfirmed inputs

questions.question: Why do most action games use UDP instead of TCP for gameplay traffic? Describe two specific problems TCP causes and how a custom reliability layer on UDP solves them while avoiding those problems.
questions.answer: Problem 1: Head-of-line blocking. TCP guarantees ordered delivery, so if packet 5 is lost, packets 6, 7, and 8 are buffered until packet 5 is retransmitted and arrives. For position updates, this adds 100-300ms of delay for data that is already obsolete (packet 8 has newer positions). Problem 2: Nagle's algorithm. TCP batches small writes into larger packets to reduce overhead, adding up to 200ms of delay before sending. Disabling it (TCP_NODELAY) helps but does not eliminate the ordered delivery problem. A custom UDP reliability layer sends position updates unreliably -- if one is lost, the next one supersedes it anyway. For important messages (player joined, item picked up), the layer adds sequence numbers and acknowledgment bitmasks: the sender retransmits unacknowledged messages, but does not block newer messages from being processed immediately. This selective reliability gives games both low latency (unreliable position) and guaranteed delivery (reliable events) without the all-or-nothing tradeoff of TCP.

rubric.key: Correctly describes head-of-line blocking in TCP
Explains Nagle's algorithm or another TCP-specific problem
Describes selective reliability with sequence numbers and acknowledgments

questions.question: Describe entity interpolation for rendering remote players in a networked game. What is the interpolation delay, why is it necessary, and what happens when packets are lost?
questions.answer: Entity interpolation renders remote entities at a time slightly in the past (typically 100-150ms behind real time), smoothly blending between the two most recent server snapshots. The interpolation delay (usually 2-3 snapshot intervals at 20 Hz) is necessary to ensure the client always has two snapshots to interpolate between. Without the delay, the client would run out of snapshots to blend whenever a single packet arrived late or was dropped, causing entities to freeze or teleport. When a packet is lost, the client still has the buffer of previous snapshots to interpolate through, smoothly degrading over the delay window. If multiple consecutive packets are lost (exceeding the buffer), the client must extrapolate using the last known velocity, which is less accurate and may produce visible corrections when new snapshots finally arrive.

rubric.key: Correctly describes interpolation between two snapshots at past time
Explains why the delay buffer is necessary (jitter/loss tolerance)
Describes fallback to extrapolation on extended packet loss

questions.question: Describe the complete pipeline for implementing a bloom post-processing effect. Include the specific shader passes, how the bright pixels are extracted, and why separable Gaussian blur is used instead of a single 2D blur.
questions.answer: Step 1: Render the scene to an FBO texture. Step 2: Bright-pass extraction -- a fragment shader reads the scene texture and outputs only pixels where luminance exceeds a threshold (e.g., luma = 0.2126*R + 0.7152*G + 0.0722*B > 1.0), outputting black for everything below threshold. Step 3: Progressive downsampling -- the bright-pass result is downsampled to 1/2, 1/4, 1/8 resolution, reducing pixel count for the blur step. Step 4: Separable Gaussian blur at each mip level -- a horizontal blur pass reads the downsampled texture and blurs along the X axis, then a vertical blur pass reads the horizontal result and blurs along the Y axis. Separable blur reduces samples from O(kernel_size^2) to O(2 * kernel_size). A 9-tap kernel requires 81 texture samples in 2D but only 18 in two separable passes. Step 5: Progressive upsampling -- each mip level is upsampled and blended with the next higher resolution level. Step 6: Composite -- the final blurred bloom is added to the original scene texture, making bright areas glow.

rubric.key: Correctly describes the bright-pass extraction with luminance threshold
Explains separable Gaussian blur advantage (O(2n) vs O(n^2))
Describes the full pipeline including downsampling, blur, upsample, composite

questions.question: You profile a game scene and find it runs at 25 FPS. The CPU frame time is 12ms and the GPU frame time is 38ms. Is this game CPU-bound or GPU-bound? Describe three specific optimization strategies you would investigate to reach 60 FPS.
questions.answer: This game is GPU-bound. The CPU completes its work in 12ms and then waits for the GPU, which takes 38ms per frame. The GPU must be brought under 16.67ms for 60 FPS. Three optimization strategies: (1) Reduce shader complexity for distant objects using shader LOD -- skip normal mapping, use simpler lighting models, or reduce texture resolution for objects far from the camera. (2) Enable aggressive frustum and occlusion culling -- use a depth pre-pass to build an occlusion buffer, then skip all objects fully hidden behind closer geometry, reducing the number of fragments processed. (3) Reduce resolution with dynamic resolution scaling -- render at 75% resolution when the GPU frame time exceeds budget, then upscale with a sharpening filter. This directly reduces the fragment shader workload proportional to the resolution reduction (75% resolution = 56% of the pixels).

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the scene as GPU-bound with reasoning
Describes three distinct, specific GPU optimization strategies
Strategies address the correct bottleneck (GPU, not CPU)

questions.question: Explain the difference between forward rendering and deferred rendering. When would you choose deferred rendering, and what is its main limitation?
questions.answer: Forward rendering computes lighting for each fragment during the geometry pass: every object's fragment shader evaluates all lights affecting it. With N objects and L lights, the cost is O(N * L) because every fragment tests against every light. Deferred rendering separates geometry from lighting: the geometry pass writes surface properties (position, normal, albedo, material) to a G-buffer of textures, then a lighting pass reads the G-buffer and computes lighting per-pixel once. The cost is O(N + P * L) where P is the number of pixels, making it ideal for scenes with many dynamic lights (e.g., a dungeon with 50 torches). The main limitation is transparency: deferred rendering writes one set of surface properties per pixel, so it cannot naturally represent semi-transparent objects that require blending multiple layers. Transparent objects typically require a separate forward pass composited on top of the deferred result.

rubric.key: Correctly describes forward rendering cost model (N * L)
Correctly describes deferred rendering G-buffer approach
Identifies transparency as the main limitation

major_assignments.title: ECS Game Engine with A* Pathfinding and Spatial Partitioning
major_assignments.description: Build a working 2D game engine using the Entity-Component-System architecture developed in class. The engine must support entity creation/destruction with generation counters, component attachment/removal, and at least five systems: Movement, Collision (with spatial partitioning), Rendering, AI Pathfinding (A* on a grid or NavMesh), and a State Machine system for character behavior. Demonstrate the engine with a scene containing a player-controlled entity with HSM-based controls, 50+ AI entities that pathfind toward the player, static obstacles, and at least one enemy type with FSM-driven behavior (patrol, chase, attack states).
objectives: Demonstrate mastery of ECS architecture with clean separation of data and logic
Implement A* pathfinding that correctly handles obstacles and dynamic re-pathing

Replace all heap allocations for frequently created/destroyed entities (bullets, particles, NPCs) with pool allocators. Demonstrate the improvements with a stress-test scene (1000+ entities with rapid spawn/destroy) and profiling data comparing single-threaded vs multi-threaded frame times.
objectives: Implement pool allocators that eliminate heap allocation during gameplay

major_assignments.title: Networked Multiplayer with Shader Effects

Implement a client-server architecture where two clients connect to an authoritative server, each controlling an entity. Clients use client-side prediction with server reconciliation for responsive local movement, and entity interpolation for smooth remote player rendering. Add at least two custom shader effects: one lighting/material effect (Blinn-Phong, normal mapping, or cel shading) and one post-processing effect (bloom, SSAO, or color grading). course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lecture and lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences will result in a 5% final grade deduction per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 3 days late. Extensions require written request at least 48 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: You may discuss approaches with classmates but all code must be written individually. Shared code between students will be treated as an academic integrity violation for all parties involved.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be your own work or properly attributed with a comment citing the source. Use of AI code generation tools must be disclosed in your documentation. Unattributed code reuse will result in a zero on the assignment.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a computer with a C++ compiler (Clang 16+ or MSVC 2022), CMake 3.25+, and a GPU supporting OpenGL 4.5 or DirectX 11. Lab machines are available but personal machines are strongly recommended.
